parsed.justification: The cycle is: real_10y -> dxy -> oil_wti -> cpi_yoy -> fed_funds -> breakeven_10y -> real_10y. To break it, I need to remove or reverse one edge. Evaluating the causal defensibility of each edge: (1) fed_funds -> breakeven_10y is well-established — the Fed's policy stance directly shapes inflation expectations through credibility channels. (2) cpi_yoy -> fed_funds is the textbook Taylor-rule relationship — the Fed reacts to realized inflation. (3) oil_wti -> cpi_yoy is a direct cost-push mechanism. (4) breakeven_10y -> real_10y reflects the accounting identity: nominal yield = real_10y + breakeven_10y, so when breakeven rises and nominal yield is sticky, real_10y falls mechanically — this is a genuine bidirectional candidate where the accounting identity direction (breakeven -> real) is actually the stronger one given that TIPS yields are derived from nominal yields minus inflation compensation. (5) dxy -> oil_wti is also a bidirectional candidate where the dollar-pricing mechanism is well-documented. The weakest directional claim is real_10y -> dxy. While higher real yields do attract capital and strengthen the dollar (interest-rate parity), this relationship is more indirect and operates through portfolio flows at a lag. The reverse direction (dxy -> real_10y) is also plausible: a stronger dollar reduces import prices and inflation, which can compress nominal yields and thus affect real yields. More importantly, the dxy level is driven by a broad set of global factors beyond U.S. real rates alone (ECB policy, global risk sentiment, trade flows), making dxy largely exogenous to real_10y in the short run. Reversing this edge to dxy -> real_10y is causally defensible: a stronger dollar compresses commodity prices and imported inflation, which reduces breakeven inflation and can push real yields higher as nominal yields adjust. This reversal breaks the cycle while preserving the most causally robust edges in the loop.
